title: Today's AI Brief: The AI Hype Index
summary: AI gets booed in graduation season, Railway secures $100 million for AI-native cloud infrastructure
imageAlt: Image from source article for The AI Hype Index: AI gets booed in graduation season
imageCaption: The AI Hype Index: AI gets booed in graduation season
imageSourceTitle: The AI Hype Index: AI gets booed in graduation season

body.text: I'm looking at a fascinating article from MIT Technology Review, which suggests that the AI hype might be hitting a wall. Former Google CEO Eric Schmidt recently told University of Arizona graduates that their task is to help shape the future of AI. However, instead of applause, he got booed. Ouch! It seems that the class of 2026 is a bit more skeptical about AI's promises than we expected. I've got to wonder, are they just jaded or is there something more to it?

In other news, Railway, a San Francisco-based cloud platform, has secured $100 million in funding to challenge AWS with AI-native cloud infrastructure. What's interesting here is that Railway has managed to amass two million developers without spending a dollar on marketing. That's like having a viral product without trying - a pretty impressive feat. I'm curious to see how they'll use this funding to take on the big players.

The AI Hype Index: AI gets booed in graduation season is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: It is one thing to say AI will change the world. It is another to expect the class of 2026 to applaud it. In fact, when former Google CEO Eric Schmidt told University of Arizona graduates that their task is to help shape AI, he was met with a resounding chorus of boos. “I can hear you,” he said, before conceding that fears about disappearing jobs and a brok... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

Railway secures $100 million to challenge AWS with AI-native cloud infrastructure is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: Railway , a San Francisco-based cloud platform that has quietly amassed two million developers without spending a dollar on marketing, announced Thursday that it raised $100 million in a Series B funding round, as surging demand for artificial intelligence applications exposes the limitations of legacy cloud infrastructure. TQ Ventures led the round, with p... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.
